title: Yo-yo biscuits
subtitle: Australian custard-flavoured sandwich biscuits
excerpt: Yo-yo biscuits are a classic Australian biscuit: soft, pale, custard-flavoured rounds sandwiched with a thick custard buttercream. They are made with a high proportion of custard powder, which gives both the biscuit and the filling their characteristic pale yellow colour and vanilla-custard flavour. They are not crisp like a shortbread; they have a soft, melt-in-the-mouth crumb. They are better the next day once the filling softens the biscuit slightly.
sourceNotes: Yo-yo biscuits are an Australian home baking staple associated with the Women's Weekly cookbooks from the 1950s onwards. They are sometimes called kisses, and versions appear in New Zealand baking as well. The custard powder content is higher than in most biscuits, which is what produces the distinctive soft, starchy crumb. The name refers to the round shape of the sandwiched biscuit pair, which resembles the toy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container for 5 days. Fill on the day or the day before — the filling softens the biscuit slightly and improves the texture.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; low temperature keeps the pale colour

content.text: The dough is very soft; it is rolled into balls and pressed flat with a fork rather than rolled and cut. Do not bake these past very pale golden; the custard powder burns easily and overbaked yo-yos lose their softness. They should be barely coloured at the edges and just set when they come out. They firm up quickly on the cooling rack.

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays. Beat 200 g softened butter with 60 g icing sugar and 1 tsp vanilla extract until pale and fluffy. Sift in 200 g flour and 80 g custard powder. Mix to a soft dough. Roll level teaspoons of dough into balls and place well apart on the lined trays. Flatten each ball gently with a fork, pressing a cross pattern. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until just barely golden at the edges; they should remain pale. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool completely.

Beat 80 g softened butter until pale. Beat in 25 g custard powder, 120 g sifted icing sugar, and 0.5 tsp vanilla until smooth. Match biscuits in pairs. Place a small teaspoon of filling on the flat side of one biscuit of each pair and press together gently.

items.problem: Biscuits browned too quickly and taste bitter
items.solution: The oven was too hot or the custard powder brand has a higher cornflour-to-colour ratio. Reduce the temperature to 155 degrees C and check at 12 minutes. The biscuits should be barely coloured — white to very faint gold.

items.problem: Biscuits crumbled when removed from the tray
items.solution: They were moved before they firmed up. Custard-heavy biscuits are fragile when hot. Leave on the tray for at least 5 minutes before lifting.
Beat in 1 teaspoon of milk to loosen slightly. The filling should be soft enough to spread without tearing the biscuit.
